---
title: "R Episode 6 — Subsetting Data"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Objectives
By the end of this episode you will be able to:

- Subset vectors by position, name, and logical condition.
- Subset data frames by rows and columns.
- Understand the difference between `[`, `[[`, and `$`.

**Teaching:** 35 min · **Exercises:** 25 min
:::

## Subsetting vectors

```r
x <- c(a = 5.4, b = 6.2, c = 7.1, d = 4.8)

x[1]              # by position
x[c(1, 3)]        # multiple positions
x[-1]             # drop the first element
x["b"]            # by name
x[x > 6]          # by logical condition  -> b, c
```

Logical subsetting is the workhorse — build a condition, use it to filter:

```r
big <- x > 6
x[big]
```

## Subsetting data frames

A data frame is two-dimensional: `df[rows, cols]`.

```r
gapminder[1:3, ]            # first 3 rows, all columns
gapminder[, c("country", "year")]   # all rows, two columns
gapminder[gapminder$year == 2007, ] # rows matching a condition
```

`$` and `[[` extract a single column as a vector; `[` keeps it a data frame:

```r
gapminder$lifeExp          # a numeric vector
gapminder[["lifeExp"]]     # same vector
gapminder["lifeExp"]       # a one-column data frame
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Instructor note
The `[` vs `[[` distinction confuses everyone at first: `[` keeps the container type, `[[` reaches
*inside* to pull one element out. Most "why is this still a data frame?" bugs trace back here.
Note that Episode 12 (dplyr) gives a more readable way to do all of this with `filter()`/`select()`.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Challenge — Filter by condition
From `gapminder`, select the rows for Norway. Then select only the `year` and `lifeExp` columns
for those rows.

### Solution

```r
norway <- gapminder[gapminder$country == "Norway", ]
norway[, c("year", "lifeExp")]
```
(In Episode 12 this becomes `gapminder |> filter(country == "Norway") |> select(year, lifeExp)`.)
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Key points
- Subset vectors by position, name, or logical condition.
- Data frames use `df[rows, cols]`.
- `[` keeps the container; `[[` and `$` extract a single element/column.
:::

Next: [Episode 7 — Control Flow](r-07-control-flow.qmd).
